As a multi-part message containing both a text content item and an image content item
explanation: A vision-based prompt uses a multi-part user message that includes a text content item and an image content item together in the same message. Naming a local file path in plain text does not transmit the image, an audio item is for speech, and the image and text are submitted together rather than in separate sequential requests.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: A developer wants to include a local image file in a prompt to a vision-enabled model, but the image is not hosted at a public web address. Which approach lets the image be passed in the prompt?
options: Reference the file by its Windows drive letter and folder path
Convert the image to an audio file first
Encode the image as Base64 and submit it as a data URL
Paste the raw binary bytes directly into the text field
explanation: For a local image, you encode the file data in Base64 and pass it as a data URL in the form data:image/jpeg;base64,{image_data}. A multimodal prompt accepts either a public image URL or Base64-encoded image data. A local drive path is not accessible to the cloud service, audio conversion is unrelated, and pasting raw binary bytes into a text field is not a supported format.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: An insurance app lets adjusters upload a photo of a damaged vehicle and asks the model to write a short description of the visible damage. Which best describes what the multimodal model does with the input?
options: It interprets the uploaded image and returns a text response describing it
It converts the photo into speech audio
It generates a brand-new image of an undamaged vehicle
It encrypts the image and stores it for later retrieval
explanation: Vision-enabled models analyze the image you upload and provide a textual response to questions about it, so describing visible damage returns text. The model is interpreting an existing image, not generating a new one, not synthesizing speech, and not an encryption or storage service.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: A logistics company photographs each warehouse shelf and needs an automated way to find every package in a photo and report exactly where each one sits, returning the bounding-box coordinates of each item so a downstream app can count items and flag empty spots. Which Azure AI Vision image analysis capability best fits this requirement?
options: Image generation from a text prompt
explanation: Object detection is the right fit because it not only identifies the items in an image but also returns the bounding-box coordinates (x, y, width, height) for each one, which is exactly what is needed to know where every package sits and to count multiple instances. Image tagging is wrong because it only returns content labels for what appears in the image without any location or bounding boxes. Image generation (for example with a gpt-image model in the Foundry portal) is wrong because it creates a new image from a text prompt rather than analyzing an existing one. OCR is wrong because it extracts printed or handwritten text from an image, but the scenario is about locating physical objects, not reading text.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: Which of the following are typical computer vision use cases for analyzing an existing image? (Select three.)
options: Generating a human-readable caption that describes the image
Detecting objects and returning their locations in the image
Extracting printed or handwritten text from the image (OCR)
Creating a brand-new image from a written description
Transcribing a recorded phone call into text
explanation: Captioning (describe), object detection (detect), and OCR (extract text) are all image-analysis tasks performed on an existing image. Creating a new image from a description is image generation, not analysis, and transcribing a phone call is speech-to-text, not computer vision.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: A publishing site wants to automatically generate a one-sentence, human-readable description of each uploaded photo to use as accessibility alt text. Which Azure AI Vision capability is designed for this?
explanation: Image Analysis captioning generates a one-sentence, human-readable description of an image's content, which is exactly what is recommended for auto-generating accessibility alt text. A text-to-image model creates new pictures from text, OCR only extracts text shown inside an image, and text-to-speech produces audio rather than a written caption.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: For each of the following statements about deployed multimodal models and image generation in Microsoft Foundry, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
statements: A vision-enabled multimodal model can accept an image together with a text prompt and respond with text about the image.
A text-to-image generation model is the correct choice when you need to analyze the contents of a photo a user uploaded.
A multimodal prompt can include an image supplied either as a web URL or as Base64-encoded image data.
explanation: Statement 1 is true: multimodal vision-enabled models take image plus text input and return text. Statement 2 is false: analyzing an uploaded photo calls for a vision-enabled (analysis) model, not a text-to-image generation model, which creates new images. Statement 3 is true: an image in a prompt can be a public URL or Base64-encoded data.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: A solution must read text printed on photographs of street signs and storefronts taken in the field, where the images are not formal documents. Which capability is purpose-built for this lightweight, in-the-wild text extraction?
options: Azure AI Vision Image Analysis Read (OCR)
Sentiment analysis in Azure AI Language
explanation: The Azure AI Vision Image Analysis Read (OCR) feature is designed to extract text from in-the-wild, non-document images such as street signs, posters, and storefronts using a fast synchronous API. Image generation creates new images, text-to-speech produces audio, and sentiment analysis evaluates text tone, none of which extract text from images.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: An online catalog already stores millions of product photos. The team wants to extract printed text and detect objects within those existing photos, and they specifically do not need to create any new images. Which Azure AI service is the right fit?
options: An image-generation model such as gpt-image-1
Azure AI Vision (image analysis and OCR)
explanation: Azure AI Vision provides image analysis and OCR to extract text and detect objects in existing images, which matches a workload that processes photos the team already has. An image-generation model creates new images rather than analyzing existing ones, Azure AI Speech handles audio, and Azure AI Language analyzes text, not images.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: A developer is building a vision-enabled chat application that lets users upload a product photo and ask a question about it. The app must send the image and question to a multimodal model in Microsoft Foundry and display the model's answer. Arrange these required development steps in the correct order, from first to last.
options: Build a client app that connects to the model endpoint and submits a prompt containing both text and an image
Deploy a multimodal (vision-enabled) model in the Foundry portal
The app processes the model's text response and displays it to the user
The multimodal model analyzes the prompt and returns a text response
explanation: The required order is fixed by data dependencies. (1) Deploy a multimodal (vision-enabled) model in the Foundry portal first, because the client app needs an existing deployment endpoint to connect to. (0) Build the client app that connects to that endpoint and submits a multi-part prompt containing both a text content item and an image content item. (3) The model then analyzes the combined text-and-image prompt and returns a text response. (2) Finally, the app processes that returned text response and displays it to the user. Each step strictly depends on the prior one: you cannot connect an app to a model that is not yet deployed, the model cannot respond before a prompt is submitted, and the app cannot process a response that has not been returned. The optional 'test in the chat playground' step was deliberately removed because validating in the playground can happen anytime after deployment (or be skipped entirely), so including it would make the ordering non-unique; the remaining four steps have one and only one valid sequence.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: A product manager asks whether the standard image-generation API used in Foundry can also crop or retouch a customer's existing photo when called with only a text prompt and no input image. What is the accurate response?
options: The image-generation API only edits existing images and cannot create new ones
The image-generation API requires an uploaded document to function
The image-generation API creates an image from a text prompt; it does not edit existing images in that basic call
The image-generation API transcribes audio into images
explanation: The image-generation API creates an image from a text prompt and, in that basic form, does not edit or create variations of an existing image. It is not limited to editing only, it does not transcribe audio, and it does not require an uploaded document.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: For each of the following statements about computer vision tasks and choosing the right capability, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
statements: Detecting where objects appear in an image and returning their bounding-box locations is an image-analysis task.
Producing a one-sentence caption that describes the overall content of an image is an image-analysis task.
Creating a new product photo from the sentence 'a red sports car on a beach' is an image-analysis task.
explanation: Object detection with locations and image captioning are both analysis tasks performed on an existing image, so the first two statements are true. Creating a new image from a text description is image generation, not analysis, so the third statement is false.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: A team has deployed an image-generation model in Microsoft Foundry and is generating product visuals from text prompts. For a time-sensitive batch where they need each picture back as fast as possible and can accept a less detailed result, which image-generation output setting should they adjust to reduce how long each generation takes?
options: Set the background setting to transparent
Set the size to the largest available dimensions, such as 1536x1024
Set the quality setting to a lower value, such as low
Set the output format to PNG instead of JPEG
explanation: Image-generation models in Microsoft Foundry expose a quality setting (for example low, medium, high, or auto), and generation time varies based on the quality setting and prompt complexity. For time-sensitive applications, the documentation recommends using a lower quality value such as low (or enabling partial images for streaming) so each image comes back faster, at the cost of less detail. The other settings do not reduce generation time for this goal: choosing the largest size generally adds work rather than speeding things up; the background setting (transparent, opaque, or auto) controls whether the image has a transparent background, not speed; and the output format (png, webp, or jpeg) controls the encoding of the returned file, not how long the model takes to generate the picture.

module: Computer Vision and Image Generation with Foundry
question: A developer is building a quality-inspection assistant that sends a product photo together with a text question ("Does this item show any visible damage?") to a vision-enabled (multimodal) chat model deployed in Microsoft Foundry. Which statement about how the request is composed is correct?
options: A single multimodal prompt can combine a text part and an image part, and the model reasons over both to return a text answer
The text question and the image must be sent as two unrelated requests, because the model cannot consider them together
A vision-enabled chat model ignores any accompanying text and only describes the image
The image can only be used to generate a brand-new image, not to answer a question about the existing photo
explanation: A vision-enabled (multimodal) chat model accepts a single prompt whose content combines text and one or more images, and it reasons over both to return a text response, which is exactly what an inspection question about a photo needs. The other options are wrong: the text and image are parts of one multimodal prompt rather than two unrelated requests; the model uses the text question together with the image instead of ignoring the text; and analyzing an existing image to answer a question is the role of a multimodal chat model, which is different from a text-to-image model that creates new images.

question: A broadcast media company has a large library of recorded TV shows and wants to make each video searchable by an AI chat assistant. For every video, they need Content Understanding to automatically split it into logical scenes and produce, for each scene, an inline transcript, representative key-frame thumbnails, and a natural-language description of what is happening, packaged as RAG-ready output that can drop straight into a vector store. Which Content Understanding modality and analyzer fits this requirement?
options: An audio analyzer, which produces a speaker-diarized transcript and call summary but no visual key frames or scene descriptions
A document analyzer, which extracts key-value fields from each frame treated as a scanned page
An image analyzer, which classifies a single still photo against a custom defect schema
A video analyzer, which segments the video into scenes and outputs transcripts, key frames, and natural-language scene descriptions as RAG-ready content
explanation: Content Understanding's video modality is built for exactly this scenario. The prebuilt video analyzer automatically performs scene segmentation and, for each segment, emits an inline WebVTT transcript, ordered key-frame thumbnails, and natural-language descriptions, packaged as RAG-ready Markdown and JSON that can be loaded directly into a vector store with no post-processing. A document analyzer targets key-value field extraction from documents like invoices, not video scenes. An audio analyzer handles transcription, diarization, and summaries but has no key frames or visual scene descriptions because there is no visual track. An image analyzer works on a single still image against a schema and does not segment time-based content or produce transcripts.

question: When you build a custom analyzer in Content Understanding, what is the primary purpose of the field schema you define?
options: It sets the pricing tier and request quota for the analyzer
It specifies the structured fields to extract, including each field's value type and how its value is generated
It chooses the human reviewers who will validate every result
It selects which Azure region the analyzer is deployed to
explanation: The field schema is a formal description of the fields to extract; for each field it specifies the name, an optional description, the value type (string, number, date, object, array, and so on), and the generation method. It acts as the contract for the structured output. It does not control region selection, pricing tier, or human-reviewer assignment.

question: A team wants Content Understanding to pull the transaction date and line-item amounts directly off receipts exactly as they appear on the document. Which field generation method best fits this requirement?
explanation: The Extract method directly captures values as they appear in the input content, such as dates and line-item amounts on receipts and invoices, and it is supported for document analyzers. Generate produces new free-form values (for example, a summary), Classify assigns content to predefined categories, and Translate is not a Content Understanding field generation method.

question: A media company uses Azure AI Content Understanding to process customer testimonial videos. For each video they want one field that holds a one-paragraph plot summary. This summary is not copied verbatim from the file; the model must reason over the content and compose new text. Which field generation method should they configure for this field?
explanation: Content Understanding supports exactly three field generation methods: Extract, Classify, and Generate. Generate is correct because it produces field values freely from the input by inference or summarization (the docs cite 'summary' as a Generate example), which is what a composed, non-verbatim one-paragraph summary requires. Extract is wrong because it returns values exactly as they appear in the content and is supported only for document analyzers, not video. Classify is wrong because it selects a value from a predefined set of categories (for example a sentiment label), not free-form text. Diarize is wrong because it is not a field generation method at all; speaker diarization is part of audio transcription.

question: A developer reads that one Content Understanding field generation method is supported only for document analyzers and is not available for image, audio, or video. Which method is described?
explanation: The Extract method, which captures values directly as they appear in the input content, is supported only for document analyzers. Generate and Classify are available across modalities, and Segment is a processing capability (dividing content into logical sections) rather than a field generation method.

question: A quality-control team on a manufacturing line wants to automatically flag product photos that show scratches, cracks, or misalignments, and record a structured defect label for each image. Which Content Understanding capability supports this?
options: Using document layout analysis to read tables out of the photos
Running a video analyzer to segment the photos into camera shots
Defining an image analyzer with a field schema to extract structured fields from images
Using an audio analyzer to transcribe the images into text
explanation: Content Understanding image solutions let you define a schema that specifies the fields to extract from images, including manufacturing quality-control scenarios such as detecting defects and anomalies like scratches, cracks, or misalignments. Audio transcription, document table extraction, and video camera-shot segmentation target other modalities and do not apply to still-image defect detection.

question: A contact center wants to process recorded calls so each call yields a transcript that distinguishes who is speaking, plus a summary and sentiment. Which Content Understanding modality and capabilities apply?
options: Document analysis with OCR and table extraction
Audio analysis with transcription and speaker diarization, plus field extraction for summary and sentiment
Image analysis with figure description and chart extraction
explanation: Content Understanding audio analyzers transcribe conversational audio and use diarization to distinguish speakers, and field extraction can produce structured outputs such as call summaries and sentiment. OCR and tables belong to document analysis, figure description and charts belong to image and document analysis, and Translator is a separate service that does not transcribe calls.

question: A bank wants to start extracting fields from US tax forms without designing a schema from scratch, because Content Understanding ships preconfigured analyzers tuned for common document categories. What are these preconfigured analyzers called?
explanation: Domain-specific (prebuilt) analyzers are preconfigured for common document categories in industries such as finance and tax, identity verification, and mortgage and lending, providing tuned field schemas out of the box. Custom analyzers require you to define or label a schema, base analyzers are the generic parents you derive custom analyzers from, and 'knowledge-store analyzer' is not a Content Understanding term.

question: An intelligent document processing workflow needs to know, for each extracted invoice field, both how reliable the value is and where in the source document it came from, so low-confidence values can be routed to human review. Which pair of Content Understanding features provides this?
explanation: Confidence scores give a 0-to-1 reliability estimate per field, and grounding identifies the source region where a value was extracted or generated; together they support straight-through processing with human review of low-confidence values. Diarization and segmentation, content filtering and guardrails, and translation and redaction address other needs and do not provide per-field reliability plus source location.

question: A startup needs an application that reads purchase orders and returns the same named fields every time so the data can be inserted into a database. A teammate suggests instead sending each file to a general chat model and asking it to 'describe the order in your own words.' Which approach better fits the requirement, and why?
options: Either one, because both always return identical structured fields
The chat model, because free-form prose is easier to insert into a relational database
The chat model, because Content Understanding cannot output structured data
Content Understanding, because a defined field schema yields consistent, schema-aligned structured output for automation
explanation: Content Understanding is built for structured extraction: a defined field schema produces predictable, schema-aligned key-value output suited to automation and database insertion. A general model asked for free-form prose returns variable text that is harder to map to fixed columns, the two approaches do not produce identical output, and Content Understanding does output structured key-value data.

question: A finance team uses Azure AI Content Understanding in the Foundry portal to extract fields from invoices. After uploading a sample invoice, defining a field schema, and confirming the test results look good, a developer selects "Build analyzer." What does building the analyzer produce that the team can use to automate invoice extraction in their own application?
options: A reusable analyzer, identified by an analyzer ID, that the application invokes by calling the Content Understanding resource endpoint and passing that analyzer ID
A separately deployed generative model that the team must publish to its own dedicated endpoint before any extraction can run
A new Azure subscription provisioned specifically to host and bill the analyzer
A one-time spreadsheet export of the fields found in the uploaded sample invoice
explanation: Building an analyzer in the Foundry portal creates a reusable analyzer that is identified by an analyzer ID. To run extraction on new files, the application calls the Content Understanding resource endpoint and specifies that analyzer ID in the request path (for example, .../contentunderstanding/analyzers/{analyzerId}). The analyzer ID is the identifier used to reference the analyzer in API calls; it is not itself a separate standalone endpoint. The keyed answer is correct because it captures both parts: build produces an analyzer (with an analyzer ID) and the app invokes it through the resource's Content Understanding API. The other options are wrong: building an analyzer does not create a separately deployed model that you must publish to its own endpoint (the analyzer runs against your existing Content Understanding resource); it is not a one-time spreadsheet export (it is a reusable configuration you call repeatedly on new files); and it does not provision a new Azure subscription (the analyzer lives within your existing Foundry/Content Understanding resource).

question: A finance team builds a custom analyzer in Azure AI Content Understanding to process invoices. Most invoices have a different number of line items, and each line item has the same fixed set of subfields: description, quantity, unit price, and amount. In the field schema, which field structure should they use to capture this variable number of repeated rows, where every row shares the same subfields?
options: A classify field with one category defined for each possible line item
A group field, represented as a single object of basic values in the API
A list field, represented as an array of single basic values in the API
A table field, represented as an array of objects in the API
explanation: Content Understanding's field schema supports nested structures. A table field models a variable number of items that each have the same fixed set of subfields, and it is represented as an array of objects of basic fields in the API. That exactly matches invoice line items, where the row count varies but every row has description, quantity, unit price, and amount. The other options do not fit: a list field is an array of single basic values of the same type (one value per item, not a set of subfields per row); a group field is a single object of related basic fields, so it captures one fixed set of values, not a variable number of repeated rows; and the classify method assigns a value from a predefined set of categories, which is not a way to represent repeating multi-field rows.

question: A retailer is planning several information-extraction projects. Which of the following are scenarios that Azure AI Content Understanding is designed to support? (Select three.)
options: Extracting vendor, date, and totals from scanned invoices into structured fields
Generating a summary and sentiment from recorded customer-support calls
Detecting defects and counting products in shelf photos
Routing live network packets between virtual machines
Provisioning and patching the underlying Windows virtual machines
explanation: Content Understanding extracts structured fields from documents (invoices), produces summaries and sentiment from audio (call recordings), and analyzes images for use cases such as shelf analysis and manufacturing defect detection. Routing network packets and provisioning or patching virtual machines are infrastructure tasks unrelated to multimodal content extraction.

question: When defining a field in a Content Understanding field schema, which of the following are properties you specify for that field? (Select two.)
options: The value type, such as string, number, date, object, or array
The generation method, such as extract, classify, or generate
The Microsoft Entra ID group allowed to view the field
The physical storage account where the source file is archived
explanation: Each field in the schema specifies a name, a value type (string, number, boolean, date, object, array), an optional description, and a generation method (extract, classify, or generate). An identity group for viewing the field and a storage account for archiving the source file are not part of the field definition.

question: A bank is enabling confidence scores and grounding for an intelligent document processing workflow. Which statements about these Content Understanding features are correct? (Select two.)
options: Confidence scores are reliability estimates ranging from 0 to 1 for extracted field values
Grounding identifies the source region in the content where a value was extracted or generated
Confidence scores are only available for the audio modality
Grounding replaces the need to define a field schema
explanation: Confidence scores estimate reliability on a 0-to-1 scale per field, and grounding traces each value back to its source region so reviewers can verify it. Confidence scores are supported for the document modality, not for audio only, and grounding is a verification aid that does not remove the need to define a field schema.

question: A solution architect is describing Azure AI Content Understanding to stakeholders. For each of the following statements, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
statements: Content Understanding can process documents, images, audio, and video within a single service.
Content Understanding transforms unstructured content into structured output based on a user-defined field schema.
Content Understanding only works with plain text input and cannot process audio or video.
explanation: Statement 1 is true: Content Understanding is multimodal and handles documents, images, audio, and video in one service. Statement 2 is true: it converts unstructured content into structured key-value output defined by a field schema. Statement 3 is false: it explicitly supports audio and video as well as documents and images, not just plain text.

question: A developer is comparing the field generation methods in Content Understanding. For each of the following statements, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
statements: The Extract method captures values directly as they appear in the input content.
The Classify method assigns content to a predefined set of categories.
The Generate method is the only method that can copy a value verbatim from a document.
explanation: Statement 1 is true: Extract directly captures values as they appear (for example, an invoice date), and it is supported for documents. Statement 2 is true: Classify selects from a predefined set of categories, such as call sentiment or document type. Statement 3 is false: copying a value verbatim is the role of the Extract method, whereas Generate produces new values by reasoning over the content.

question: You are building a single-file invoice-extraction task in the Foundry portal using Content Understanding. Put the following steps in the correct order, from first to last.
options: Define the field schema specifying the fields to extract
Select Build analyzer to create a reusable analyzer (analyzer ID)
Call the analyzer from your application to process new invoices
explanation: The Foundry workflow starts by uploading a sample file, then defining and saving the field schema that specifies what to extract, then selecting Build analyzer to create a reusable analyzer that is identified by an analyzer ID, and finally calling that analyzer from your application by invoking the Content Understanding resource endpoint and passing the analyzer ID to process new files. Building an analyzer does not create a separate standalone endpoint; the analyzer runs against your existing Content Understanding resource, and the analyzer ID is the identifier you pass in the API call.
